🏢 Buyer Org Chart VERTICAL-SPECIFIC · BUDGET MAPPING · WHO TO MULTI-THREAD
Interactive org charts showing who controls budget, who champions, who blocks — by vertical. Based on Morley's action item from the Apr 9 feedback session.
👤Buyer Personas6 KEY PERSONAS · DEEP PROFILES
Comprehensive buyer persona profiles with day-to-day priorities, buying triggers, objection patterns, and Orion-specific value propositions. Built from industry research across 500+ enterprise security organizations.
👔
CISO — Chief Information Security Officer
C-Suite · Budget Holder · $250K-$600K+ comp · Reports to CEO/CIO/Board
📋 Role & Day-to-Day

The CISO is the C-suite executive responsible for enterprise security strategy, bridging technical security operations with business risk management and regulatory compliance. Their day splits between board/executive communication (translating technical risk into business impact), vendor evaluation, incident response oversight, and cross-functional alignment with legal, compliance, and engineering. They manage $2M-$50M+ security budgets and teams of 10-200+ across SOC, threat intel, GRC, and application security.

🔥 Pain Points

1. Communicating Security ROI to the Board — Leadership views security as a cost center. Needs business-impact language, not technical metrics.
2. Tool Sprawl & Integration Fatigue — Average enterprise runs 76 security tools. Each new vendor adds complexity without consolidating investigation workflow.
3. Incident Attribution Speed — Board asks "who attacked us?" within hours. Current MTTA (mean time to attribution) is 14+ days with manual investigation.
4. Regulatory Pressure Acceleration — SEC 4-day disclosure, DORA, NIS2, CIRCIA all mandate faster investigation and reporting. Compliance timelines are shrinking.
5. Talent Shortage — 3.5M+ unfilled cybersecurity positions globally. Can't hire fast enough, so needs force-multiplier technology.
6. Third-Party & Supply Chain Risk — No visibility into dark web threats targeting vendors and partners in their supply chain.

📊 Key Metrics They Own

• Mean Time to Detect (MTTD) & Mean Time to Respond (MTTR)
• Mean Time to Attribution (MTTA) — critical for SEC/DORA reporting
• Security spend as % of IT budget (benchmark: 10-15%)
• Incident cost reduction year-over-year
• SOC analyst productivity (cases closed per analyst per day)
• Compliance audit pass rate and remediation velocity

⚡ Buying Triggers

• Recent breach or near-miss incident exposing investigation gaps
• New regulatory mandate (DORA, NIS2, SEC cyber rules) with hard deadlines
• Board-level mandate for improved threat intelligence and attribution
• Competitive pressure — peer CISO adopted AI investigation
• M&A integration requiring unified security platform
• Major vendor contract renewal creating evaluation window

🧠 Decision-Making Style

ROI-driven evaluation with 90-day POC. Requires peer references from similar-size organizations. Needs board-ready business case. Consults Gartner/Forrester. Values vendor stability and roadmap. Typical sales cycle: 6-12 months. Budget authority: $500K-$5M+.

🎯 Why Orion Resonates

1. Board-Ready Attribution — AI-generated investigation reports that translate technical findings into business impact language CISOs can present to the board
2. Tool Consolidation — Replace 3-5 point solutions (dark web monitor + threat intel feed + investigation platform + blockchain tracer) with one AI platform
3. MTTA Reduction — Reduce attribution from 14 days to hours with AI-driven entity resolution and threat actor profiling
4. Regulatory Compliance — Investigation audit trails for SEC, DORA, NIS2 — the documentation regulators require

🔍 Discovery Questions

• "When your SOC escalates a critical incident, how quickly can your team attribute it to a specific threat group?"
• "How many separate tools does your investigation workflow currently require?"
• "How do you currently demonstrate investigation capability for SEC/DORA/NIS2 auditors?"
• "What's your dark web visibility for threats targeting your organization specifically?"
• "If your board asked tomorrow 'are we being discussed on the dark web,' could you answer in real-time?"

🛡️ Common Objections

• "We already have [Recorded Future/CrowdStrike]" → Those detect. Orion investigates. Different layer.
• "Budget is committed" → Position under new regulatory mandate (emergency procurement)
• "Too many tools already" → Orion consolidates 3-5 tools. Show the consolidation math.
• "Need to see ROI first" → Offer 90-day POC + ROI calculator with their actual numbers

ENGAGEMENT INTEL: Reads: WSJ CIO Journal, Dark Reading, CSO Online · Attends: RSA, Black Hat, Gartner Security Summit · Reports to: CEO (48%), CIO (31%), Board (21%) · Avg tenure: 26 months · Top concern 2025-26: AI-powered attacks + regulatory compliance convergence
💻
CTO / VP Engineering
C-Suite/VP · Technical Authority · $200K-$500K+ comp · Reports to CEO/COO
📋 Role & Day-to-Day

The CTO/VP Engineering owns technology strategy, architecture decisions, and engineering execution. They split time between product/platform architecture, engineering team management, vendor technical evaluation, and security-engineering alignment. In fintech and crypto companies, they're often the primary security decision-maker. They care deeply about API security, infrastructure integrity, developer productivity, and build-vs-buy economics. They evaluate tools through a technical lens — architecture, API quality, integration effort, and scalability.

🔥 Pain Points

1. Supply Chain & Dependency Risk — SolarWinds, Log4j, and MOVEit showed the devastation of supply chain attacks. No visibility into compromised dependencies until it's too late.
2. API Attack Surface Explosion — Microservices architecture = thousands of API endpoints. Each is a potential attack vector, but dark web threat context is missing from AppSec tools.
3. Speed vs. Security Tension — Engineering wants to ship fast. Security wants to scan everything. Needs threat intelligence integrated into CI/CD without slowing deployment.
4. Credential & Secret Exposure — Developers accidentally commit API keys, tokens, and credentials to public repos. Dark web marketplaces trade stolen developer credentials.
5. Build vs. Buy Fatigue — Internal security tooling consumes engineering resources that should be building product. Needs platforms that are API-first and engineer-friendly.

📊 Key Metrics

• Deployment frequency & change failure rate (DORA metrics)
• Mean time to remediate critical vulnerabilities
• API uptime and incident impact on SLAs
• Engineering time spent on security vs. product development
• Third-party dependency risk score

⚡ Buying Triggers

• Supply chain incident impacting their stack or a competitor
• Developer credentials found on dark web marketplace
• Board/CISO mandate for proactive threat intelligence in engineering
• Crypto/DeFi company experiencing or fearing hack
• Migration to cloud-native/microservices creating new attack surface

🎯 Why Orion Resonates

1. API-First Architecture — RESTful APIs, webhooks, and SIEM integrations. Engineer-friendly, not analyst-only.
2. Dark Web Dev Credential Monitoring — Detect when developer credentials, API keys, or source code appears on underground markets
3. Supply Chain Threat Intel — Monitor dark web for discussions of exploits targeting your specific technology dependencies
4. Crypto/DeFi Investigation — For fintech CTOs: blockchain tracing, smart contract exploit investigation, protocol-level threat intelligence

ENGAGEMENT INTEL: Reads: Hacker News, The Pragmatic Engineer, TLDR Security · Attends: DEF CON, KubeCon, Web Summit · Decision style: Technical POC first, then ROI. Wants to see API docs and architecture before a demo. · Budget influence: $500K-$5M for security tooling · Skeptical of vendor claims — show, don't tell.
⚖️
Chief Compliance Officer (CCO)
C-Suite · Regulatory Authority · $200K-$450K+ comp · Reports to CEO/Board/General Counsel
📋 Role & Day-to-Day

The CCO oversees regulatory compliance, risk management, and audit readiness across the organization. In financial services, they manage AML/KYC programs, sanctions screening, and regulatory examinations. In regulated industries (healthcare, energy, government contractors), they ensure compliance with sector-specific mandates. Their day involves regulatory interpretation, policy development, audit preparation, examiner relationships, and cross-functional coordination with legal, security, and business units. They're the primary contact for regulators and examiners.

🔥 Pain Points

1. Regulatory Tsunami — DORA, NIS2, SEC cyber rules, MiCA, CIRCIA all hit simultaneously. Each requires documented investigation capability they may not have.
2. Cyber-Financial Crime Convergence — Regulators now expect unified investigation of threats that span cyber (ransomware) and financial (crypto laundering). Siloed teams can't deliver this.
3. Audit Trail Gaps — Examiners want evidence of investigation process, not just detection. Current tools don't generate compliance-ready documentation.
4. SAR/STR Accuracy Burden — False positive rates of 95%+ in transaction monitoring. Each alert requires investigation context CCOs struggle to get from cyber teams.
5. Regulatory Examination Anxiety — Fear of enforcement action for inadequate investigation capability. Personal liability for compliance failures.

📊 Key Metrics

• Regulatory examination outcomes (MRA/MRIA count)
• SAR/STR filing accuracy and timeliness
• Compliance program maturity score
• Time to produce investigation evidence for examiners
• Sanctions screening false positive rate

⚡ Buying Triggers

• Regulatory examination finding (MRA) for investigation gaps
• New regulation with hard compliance deadline (DORA Jan 2025)
• Peer institution fined for inadequate investigation capability
• Merger requiring unified compliance framework
• Crypto business line creating new regulatory obligations

🎯 Why Orion Resonates

1. Regulatory Investigation Documentation — Automatic audit trails that satisfy DORA, NIS2, and SEC investigation requirements
2. AML-Cyber Convergence — Single platform bridging financial crime (sanctions, SAR) and cyber investigation (dark web, threat actors). Exactly what examiners want to see.
3. Crypto Compliance — VASP supervision, blockchain tracing, sanctions screening for digital asset exposure
4. Examiner-Ready Reports — AI-generated investigation summaries formatted for regulatory examination

ENGAGEMENT INTEL: Reads: ACAMS Today, Thomson Reuters Regulatory Intelligence, Compliance Week · Attends: ACAMS, SIFMA Compliance Conference, Money20/20 · Decision style: Risk-averse, regulatory alignment first. Needs proof of regulatory acceptance. Values case studies from peer institutions. · Budget influence: $1M-$10M for compliance technology · Personal motivation: Avoid personal liability and enforcement action
🖥️
SOC Manager / Security Operations Analyst
Operations · Technical Champion · $90K-$200K comp · Reports to CISO/Director of Security
📋 Role & Day-to-Day

The SOC Manager leads the security operations center — the 24/7 frontline of organizational defense. They manage Tier 1-3 analysts, triage alert queues, coordinate incident escalation, maintain detection rules, and ensure SLA compliance. SOC Analysts (their team) spend 80% of their day in SIEM/SOAR platforms investigating alerts. The #1 frustration: lack of context. An alert fires, but WHO is the threat actor? Are they targeting us specifically? What's their infrastructure? Analysts pivot between 5-8 tools trying to answer questions Orion answers in seconds.

🔥 Pain Points

1. Alert Fatigue & False Positive Overload — Average SOC receives 10,000+ alerts/day. 45% are false positives. Analysts waste hours on noise because they lack context to quickly qualify or dismiss.
2. Context Switching Between Tools — Investigation requires pivoting between SIEM, TIP, OSINT tools, dark web monitors, and manual research. Average investigation touches 6.3 different tools.
3. Analyst Burnout & Retention — SOC analyst turnover exceeds 30% annually. Repetitive alert triage and tool frustration drive talent away. The best analysts leave first.
4. Attribution Gap — SIEM tells you WHAT happened. SOC needs to know WHO did it. Manual attribution takes days-to-weeks and often fails entirely.
5. Dark Web Blind Spot — When an analyst sees suspicious IOCs, they have no way to check if those IOCs map to known threat actors on the dark web without separate tooling.

📊 Key Metrics

• Mean Time to Detect (MTTD) — target: <24 hours
• Mean Time to Respond (MTTR) — target: <4 hours
• Alert-to-case conversion rate (quality of triage)
• Cases closed per analyst per day
• SLA compliance rate for incident response
• False positive rate reduction month-over-month

⚡ Buying Triggers

• Critical incident where manual investigation took too long
• Analyst resignation citing tool frustration / burnout
• CISO mandate to improve MTTD/MTTR metrics
• Failed red team exercise exposing investigation gaps
• SIEM contract renewal creating evaluation window

🎯 Why Orion Resonates

1. Instant IOC Enrichment — Right-click any IOC in SIEM, get instant Orion context: threat actor, dark web mentions, infrastructure map, risk score. Seconds, not hours.
2. Unified Investigation Pane — Dark web + OSINT + blockchain + threat intel in one interface. Stop switching between 6 tools.
3. Analyst Force Multiplier — AI-driven investigation means junior analysts produce senior-level attribution. Helps with staffing crisis.
4. SIEM/SOAR Integration — Bi-directional API with Splunk, Sentinel, QRadar, Cortex XSOAR. Orion enriches existing workflows.

ENGAGEMENT INTEL: Reads: SANS newsletters, r/netsec, Krebs on Security, BleepingComputer · Attends: SANS summits, BSides, Blue Team Village at DEF CON · Decision style: Hands-on POC — will spend 2 weeks testing tool themselves. Needs to see integration with their SIEM. Values speed and UI quality. · Budget influence: $100K-$500K (recommends to CISO) · This persona is your TECHNICAL CHAMPION. Win them and they'll sell Orion internally.
🔍
Threat Intelligence Analyst / Threat Hunter
Advanced Ops · Research-Driven · $120K-$250K comp · Reports to CISO/SOC Director/CTI Lead
📋 Role & Day-to-Day

Threat hunters and CTI analysts are the most technically sophisticated buyers. They proactively search for threats that evade automated detection, track adversary campaigns, develop threat models, produce finished intelligence products, and create detection signatures. They live in the data — dark web forums, malware samples, network telemetry, and threat intelligence feeds. They evaluate tools based on intelligence depth, not dashboards. They're the hardest to impress and the most valuable champions. If a threat hunter recommends Orion, the CISO listens.

🔥 Pain Points

1. Intelligence Depth vs. Volume — Threat intel feeds deliver millions of IOCs but lack the context that makes them actionable. "Coverage without depth is noise."
2. Dark Web Access Limitations — Gaining and maintaining access to closed forums, invite-only marketplaces, and encrypted Telegram channels is operationally expensive and risky.
3. Attribution Complexity — Linking infrastructure, personas, financial flows, and TTPs to specific threat groups requires multi-source correlation that manual analysis can't scale.
4. Hunt Hypothesis Validation — Needs external intelligence to confirm or deny hypotheses about adversary behavior. Current tools provide indicators but not investigative depth.
5. Cross-Domain Correlation — Modern threat actors operate across dark web, blockchain, OSINT, and infrastructure. No single tool connects all these domains.

📊 Key Metrics

• Hunt effectiveness rate (% of hunts producing actionable findings)
• Threat actor campaigns tracked and attributed
• Detection rules created from threat intelligence
• Time from intelligence collection to finished product
• Unique dark web sources monitored

⚡ Buying Triggers

• Discovery of threat actor targeting their organization on dark web
• Failed attribution on a critical incident
• Competitor organization published superior threat research
• New threat actor group operating in their sector
• Existing threat intel vendor failing to provide depth

🎯 Why Orion Resonates

1. Multi-Hop Entity Graph — Traverse relationships between actors, infrastructure, wallets, forums, and personas. Not keyword search — actual graph traversal.
2. Dark Web Depth — 200+ marketplaces, 1000+ Telegram channels, 50+ forums in 12 languages. Collection depth that individual teams can't replicate.
3. Blockchain-Dark Web Correlation — Link crypto wallets to dark web personas to infrastructure. The cross-domain analysis that makes Orion unique.
4. C2 & Infrastructure Mapping — Map threat actor command and control infrastructure, hosting patterns, and domain registration behaviors.

ENGAGEMENT INTEL: Reads: MITRE ATT&CK blog, VirusTotal blog, Mandiant reports, Bellingcat · Attends: SANS CTI Summit, FIRST Conference, Virus Bulletin · Decision style: Data-driven skeptic. Won't be impressed by dashboards — wants to see raw intelligence quality, API access, and entity graph depth. Will test extensively. · Budget influence: $200K-$1M (strong recommendation to CISO) · WARNING: Do NOT oversell to this persona. They'll find weaknesses. Be honest about limitations.
🏦
Financial Crime Investigator / AML Analyst
Investigation & Compliance · $80K-$180K comp · Reports to BSA Officer/Head of Financial Crime/CCO
📋 Role & Day-to-Day

Financial crime investigators and AML analysts sit at the intersection of compliance and investigation. They review transaction monitoring alerts, investigate suspicious activity, file SARs/STRs, conduct enhanced due diligence, and trace financial flows. In crypto-exposed institutions, they investigate blockchain transactions, VASP compliance, and sanctions evasion. Their work is increasingly converging with cyber investigation as ransomware payments, crypto fraud, and dark web financial crime blur traditional boundaries between AML and cyber teams.

🔥 Pain Points

1. False Positive Epidemic — Transaction monitoring systems generate 95%+ false positives. Each requires investigation. The volume is crushing analyst productivity.
2. Crypto Knowledge Gap — Traditional AML analysts lack blockchain investigation skills. Crypto transactions are opaque without specialized tools.
3. Sanctions Evasion Sophistication — Nation-states (DPRK, Iran, Russia) use crypto mixers, bridges, and DeFi to evade sanctions. Traditional screening can't detect this.
4. SAR/STR Quality Pressure — Regulators increasingly scrutinize SAR quality, not just quantity. Investigations need supporting evidence from dark web and blockchain analysis.
5. Cyber-Financial Convergence — Ransomware payments, crypto fraud, and dark web financial operations span both cyber and AML domains. Siloed tools create investigation blind spots.

📊 Key Metrics

• SAR/STR filing timeliness and quality scores
• Alert-to-SAR conversion rate
• Investigation cycle time (alert to disposition)
• False positive reduction rate
• Sanctions screening match accuracy
• Crypto transaction tracing success rate

⚡ Buying Triggers

• Regulatory exam finding on investigation quality
• Crypto-related fraud case they couldn't investigate with current tools
• Sanctions penalty at a peer institution
• New crypto/VASP business line requiring investigation capability
• FinCEN/FCA/AMLA enforcement action in their sector

🎯 Why Orion Resonates

1. Blockchain Tracing — Trace crypto transactions through mixers, bridges, and DeFi protocols to attribution. Built on TRM's industry-leading blockchain intelligence.
2. Dark Web Financial Intelligence — Monitor dark web marketplaces for financial crime — stolen cards, account credentials, money mule recruitment, fraud-as-a-service.
3. Sanctions Evasion Detection — Identify DPRK, Iran, Russia sanctions evasion through crypto, with threat actor attribution. Goes beyond address screening to behavioral analysis.
4. SAR Quality Enhancement — AI-generated investigation context that makes SARs more complete and defensible for examiner review.

ENGAGEMENT INTEL: Reads: ACAMS Today, FinCEN advisories, BSA Coalition reports · Attends: ACAMS, CAMS certification events, FinCrime World · Decision style: Compliance-driven. Needs regulatory alignment proof and peer institution case studies. Values accuracy over speed — one bad SAR is worse than a late SAR. · Budget influence: $200K-$2M (recommends to BSA Officer/CCO) · KEY INSIGHT: This persona is the bridge between TRM's existing crypto compliance customers and Orion. Natural upsell path.
⚙️Orion Investigation Workflows14 ROLES · STEP-BY-STEP · TIME BENCHMARKS
Role-specific investigation workflows showing exactly where Orion eliminates manual work. Each workflow includes time benchmarks, tool handoffs, and the exact moment Orion creates the "wow" — use these to map value to the prospect's actual day, not a generic pitch.
🔬
CTI Analyst
Without Orion: 4–8 hrs/report → With Orion: 30–45 min
1. Requirement scoping — Define threat actor, campaign, or financial tracking focus. Identify relevant chains, markets, actors.
2. ▶ ORION — Parallel sweep: 200+ dark web forums + 1,000+ Telegram channels + 30+ blockchain networks simultaneously in 50+ languages. Replaces 4–6 manual tool queries.
3. ▶ ORION — Entity graph auto-built: wallet clustering, IP infrastructure, alias resolution, cross-source correlation. Identifies connections a human would miss in hours of manual pivot work.
4. TTP Analysis — Map findings to MITRE ATT&CK framework, validate actor attribution against historical CTI baseline.
5. ▶ ORION — Auto-generate sourced intelligence report: STIX-formatted IOCs, confidence scoring, evidence chain, PDF/CSV export — court/boardroom ready. Replaces 2–4 hours of write-up.
6. Dissemination — Distribute to ISAC, LE partners, leadership, or internal SOC.
⚡ Orion replaces steps 2, 3, 5 — saving 3–6 hours per intelligence product. Analyst freed for strategic analysis.
🖥️
SOC Analyst (L1/L2)
Without Orion: 45–90 min/alert → With Orion: 5–10 min
1. Alert triage — SIEM fires alert involving crypto wallet, suspicious IP, dark web mention, or blockchain entity.
2. ▶ ORION — Instant context: risk score, sanctions status, blockchain exposure, dark web mentions, stealer log hits — in seconds. No manual tool switch.
3. ▶ ORION — Actor profile: known TTPs, previous victims, affiliated infrastructure, Telegram handles, real-world identity linkage. Escalation decision made with full context.
4. Severity determination — P1/P2/P3 routing based on Orion-enriched context, not gut feel.
5. Escalation / containment — Route to L3/IR or close with full evidence in ticket.
6. ▶ ORION — Auto-document case: evidence chain, timestamps, source citations — SAR-ready, SIEM-ticket ready. Eliminates manual case note writing.
⚡ 8x analyst throughput. SOC teams using Orion process 90+ alerts/day vs. 10–15 without it.
🔍
Threat Hunter
Without Orion: 2–5 days/hunt → With Orion: 4–8 hrs
1. Hypothesis formation — "DPRK actor may have pre-positioned in our supply chain" or "ransomware affiliate targeting our sector via initial access broker."
2. ▶ ORION — Map known actor infrastructure: domains, IPs, hosting patterns, wallets — identify IOCs for your environment before they fire. Pulls from 15+ dark web sources simultaneously.
3. ▶ ORION — Multi-hop graph traversal: dark web forum post → infrastructure → financial wallet → identity — in one interface. Previously required 3–5 separate tools and 2–3 analyst days.
4. Environment validation — Push Orion-derived IOCs to SIEM/EDR for environment-specific hit analysis.
5. Detection rule creation — Convert hunt findings to Sigma/YARA/STIX detection rules with analyst review.
6. ▶ ORION — Generate hunt report: actor profile, IOC list, confidence ratings, methodology trail — ready for leadership or LE referral.
⚡ Orion collapses a 5-day hunt into 8 hours. Transforms reactive hunting into continuous proactive detection.
💰
Financial Crime Investigator (LE/FIU)
Without Orion: 3–14 days/case → With Orion: 2–6 hrs
1. Case intake — Receive referral from SAR, subpoena, victim complaint, or internal alert. Crypto wallet or transaction hash is the lead.
2. ▶ ORION — Cross-chain trace: follow funds across 30+ blockchains through mixers, bridges, DEXes — Orion auto-maps each hop with source citations. Previously required specialist blockchain analysts.
3. ▶ ORION — Exchange attribution: identify KYC-required exchange where funds landed, auto-generate subpoena routing recommendation. Converts blockchain data into actionable LE intelligence.
4. ▶ ORION — Identity linkage: cross-reference dark web mentions, stealer logs, OSINT — connect wallet to real-world identity or previously known criminal aliases.
5. Legal package preparation — Compile evidence with chain-of-custody documentation, source citations, methodology for court admissibility.
6. ▶ ORION — Auto-generate case narrative: timeline, financial flow diagram, actor attribution — court-ready in minutes, not days.
⚡ FBI/IRS-CI benchmark: Orion cuts complex crypto cases from 3 weeks to 2 days. Source citation critical for court admissibility.
🏦
AML Compliance Officer (Bank / VASP)
Without Orion: 2–6 hrs/SAR case → With Orion: 25–45 min
1. Alert intake — Transaction monitoring system flags suspicious crypto withdrawal or high-risk counterparty exposure. AML case opened.
2. ▶ ORION — Instant sanctions check: OFAC/SDN real-time match, indirect exposure calculation through multi-hop tracing. Identifies exposure the transaction monitoring system missed.
3. ▶ ORION — Counterparty risk profiling: dark web mentions, prior enforcement actions, DPRK/ransomware wallet links, known scam history. Full picture in 60 seconds.
4. SAR decision — File or no-file determination with documented rationale and supporting evidence.
5. Regulatory reporting — Submit SAR to FinCEN/FCA/AUSTRAC/MAS with full tracing evidence and narrative.
6. ▶ ORION — Auto-draft SAR narrative from investigation findings — removes the most time-consuming part of the AML workflow.
⚡ Orion turns AML investigations from multi-day bottlenecks into same-day resolutions. Reduces false positive escalations by 60%+.
🚨
Incident Responder — Ransomware
Without Orion: Days to identify actor → With Orion: Hours
1. Breach confirmed — Ransomware note received. Wallet address for payment demand identified. Clock starts on negotiation window.
2. ▶ ORION — Wallet attribution: identify ransomware gang from wallet cluster, link to known group (LockBit, ALPHV, Black Basta), pull prior victim list and ransom history. Happens in under 2 minutes.
3. ▶ ORION — Sanctions screening: confirm wallet is not OFAC-listed (Garantex, Hydra-linked wallets). Paying a sanctioned entity creates secondary legal liability — Orion flags this instantly.
4. ▶ ORION — Dark web monitoring: find group's leak site, check if victim data already posted, monitor negotiation communications on Telegram. Critical for containment decision.
5. LE referral — Compile Orion-generated evidence package for FBI/CISA submission, asset seizure coordination.
6. ▶ ORION — Track fund movement post-payment (if made) for asset recovery coordination with DOJ/FBI Cyber Division.
⚡ In active ransomware IR, minutes matter. Orion delivers attribution and sanctions risk in <5 min — faster than any manual workflow.
🕵️
Fraud Investigator (Bank / Fintech)
Without Orion: 1–3 days/complex case → With Orion: 3–6 hrs
1. Fraud alert — Customer reports unauthorized crypto withdrawal, pig butchering scam, or investment fraud. Receiving wallet identified.
2. ▶ ORION — Scam infrastructure mapping: identify pig butchering network, link to known Myanmar/Cambodia-based operation, map scale of scheme from wallet clustering.
3. ▶ ORION — Fund tracing: follow victim's funds through mixer hops to consolidation wallet or exchange. Identify if funds are still traceable/seizable.
4. Victim notification & dispute — Document evidence for chargeback process, LE referral, or civil asset recovery.
5. ▶ ORION — Generate fraud investigation report with visual financial flow, actor profile, and LE referral package. Used directly in SAR and law enforcement submission.
6. Recovery coordination — Work with LE (Secret Service, FBI, HSI) and exchanges for asset freeze requests.
⚡ Pig butchering tracing is an Orion marquee use case — SG Police, Thai RTP, and US USSS all use this workflow.
🔐
Digital Forensics / Crypto Forensics Examiner
Without Orion: 3–10 days/exam → With Orion: 1–2 days
1. Evidence intake — Seized device/wallets presented for examination. Extraction of private keys, seed phrases, transaction history.
2. ▶ ORION — Full transaction history reconstruction: complete blockchain ledger for all identified wallets across all chains. Generates visual timeline of financial activity.
3. ▶ ORION — Entity attribution: identify all counterparties, exchange accounts, and real-world identity links from blockchain evidence. Builds prosecution-ready evidence map.
4. Methodology documentation — Document examination methodology for court admissibility (chain of custody, tool validation).
5. ▶ ORION — Expert witness report generation: automated narrative with citation of data sources — critical for LE court cases and prosecutorial packages.
6. Court testimony preparation — Review Orion visualizations and timeline for presentation to jury or magistrate.
⚡ Source citations are mission-critical for LE court use — Orion's evidence chain survives Daubert/hearsay challenges better than manual workflows.
💊
Narcotics Investigator (DEA / HIDTA / SOCA)
Use case: Dark web drug markets + crypto laundering
1. Vendor/marketplace identification — Dark web marketplace vendor identified via informant, intercept, or OSINT.
2. ▶ ORION — Vendor profile extraction: marketplace listings, crypto wallet addresses, shipping patterns, customer reviews across 50+ dark web markets. Full intelligence package built automatically.
3. ▶ ORION — Financial network mapping: trace drug proceeds from marketplace wallets through mixing services to final cash-out point. Identify money laundering infrastructure and co-conspirators.
4. ▶ ORION — Identity resolution: link vendor alias to real-world identity through OSINT, shipping addresses, communication pattern analysis across platforms.
5. Undercover coordination — Use Orion intelligence to inform controlled buys, takedowns, and asset seizure warrants.
6. ▶ ORION — Affidavit support package: financial flow charts, vendor attribution documentation, marketplace activity timeline — reduces AUSA preparation time significantly.
⚡ DEA SOS uses this workflow for cross-border cartel crypto investigations. NCA/GNCCB use it for UK darknet market dismantlement.
🏛️
Intelligence Analyst (IC / NatSec)
Use case: Nation-state actor financial network disruption
1. Tasking received — Analyze DPRK/Iran/Russia financial networks supporting sanctions evasion, weapons proliferation, or influence operations.
2. ▶ ORION — Nation-state wallet cluster mapping: identify all Lazarus Group/APT38 wallets, trace heist proceeds through obfuscation layers, map the full financial infrastructure.
3. ▶ ORION — Influence operation financial tracking: trace crypto funding of disinformation campaigns, bot networks, hack-and-leak operations to state actors or proxies.
4. OFAC designation support — Compile evidence package for Treasury OFAC wallet designation action. Orion's tracing methodology satisfies designation evidentiary standard.
5. ▶ ORION — Network disruption recommendation: identify choke points in adversary financial network for maximum impact sanctions targeting.
6. Interagency coordination — Share Orion intelligence products with partner agencies (FBI, CIA, CYBERCOM, allied Five Eyes).
⚡ NSA/CIA use case: Lazarus Group financial network mapping supports both intelligence collection and active countermeasures coordination.
⚖️
Sanctions & Export Control Officer
Use case: Pre-transaction screening + post-settlement exposure
1. Transaction flagged — Large crypto transfer to/from unverified counterparty. Need to determine sanctions exposure before settlement or for SAR decision.
2. ▶ ORION — Multi-hop sanctions check: trace not just direct counterparty but 3–5 hops back through transaction history. Indirect OFAC/SDN exposure often invisible to transaction monitoring systems.
3. ▶ ORION — Exchange jurisdiction mapping: determine which exchange holds funds and its regulatory status. Identify Garantex/Blender/Tornado Cash-linked transactions triggering automatic OFAC exposure.
4. Voluntary self-disclosure — If exposure confirmed, prepare OFAC voluntary self-disclosure filing with evidence package.
5. ▶ ORION — Generate compliance documentation: methodology report, exposure calculation, remediation steps — satisfies OFAC no-action letter requirements and demonstrates good-faith compliance program.
⚡ Key differentiator vs. transaction monitoring: Orion traces indirect exposure that traditional AML systems miss. Binance, Coinbase, Kraken use this for Travel Rule compliance.
🛡️
CISO / Head of Security Operations
Use case: Board-level threat briefing + IR oversight
1. Threat briefing requirement — Board / audit committee requests threat landscape briefing relevant to sector and company profile.
2. ▶ ORION — Sector-specific threat profile: top 5 active threat actors targeting your vertical, recent TTPs, financial infrastructure of groups. Briefing-ready in minutes, not days.
3. ▶ ORION — Peer incident monitoring: identify recent attacks on peer organizations to frame "why now" urgency. Demonstrates real-world applicability to board members.
4. Risk quantification — Translate threat intelligence into financial exposure: average ransom demand for your sector, breach cost benchmarks, regulatory penalty ranges.
5. ▶ ORION — Executive report generation: board-ready threat brief with heat map, risk scoring, and recommended controls — formatted for non-technical audience. Replaces 2 days of analyst report writing.
⚡ CISO value: Orion reduces threat briefing prep from 2 analyst-days to 2 hours. Enables continuous board-level visibility vs. quarterly point-in-time reports.
🌐
OSINT Investigator (LE / Corporate)
Without Orion: 6–12 hrs/subject → With Orion: 1–2 hrs
1. Subject identification — Crypto wallet address, online alias, email address, or phone number is the investigative lead.
2. ▶ ORION — Cross-platform identity resolution: Pipl identity graph, social media correlation, domain WHOIS history, dark web forum account linkage — all from a single query.
3. ▶ ORION — Dark web presence scan: check alias across 200+ criminal forums, marketplace vendor profiles, Telegram channels. Reveals criminal history invisible to surface web OSINT.
4. ▶ ORION — Financial footprint: blockchain addresses associated with identity, exchange account linkage, prior sanctioned entity connections.
5. Identity confirmation — Corroborate findings across 3+ independent sources for legal admissibility standard.
6. ▶ ORION — Subject dossier: formatted intelligence product with all source citations, confidence ratings, and recommended next investigative steps.
⚡ Orion's unified identity graph (blockchain + dark web + OSINT) is the biggest differentiator over point OSINT tools like Maltego or Paliscope.

🧩 Orion Endpoint Library 80+ ENDPOINTS · SAMPLE RETURNS · SEARCHABLE
➕ ADD CUSTOM ENDPOINT
Personas:
🔍
📖 Orion Sales Glossary 90+ TERMS · 8 CATEGORIES · ORION-SPECIFIC
Terms reps need to know cold before any Orion demo or discovery call. Organised by what matters in the field — not generic cybersecurity 101.
🔍
🤖
Orion Platform (14)

Orion — TRM Labs' AI-native criminal investigation platform. Used by LE, financial institutions, and exchanges to investigate crypto crime, ransomware, and financial fraud. NOT a crypto trading tool.

Co-Case Agent — Orion's AI investigation agent that autonomously runs multi-step investigations. Accepts natural language queries and returns structured intelligence reports. Launched March 25 2026.

NLQ (Natural Language Query) — How analysts interact with Orion. Type "trace this wallet through mixers to its exchange cashout" and Orion runs it. No query language required.

Entity Graph — Orion's knowledge graph connecting wallets, addresses, actors, infrastructure, and dark web personas into a unified investigation picture.

Risk Score — Orion's 0–10 score for a wallet or transaction. Composed of: direct exposure to high-risk services, indirect exposure, entity classification, and volume patterns.

Chain Hop — Moving funds across multiple blockchains to obscure origin. Orion traces cross-chain automatically across 40+ chains.

Dark Web Fusion — Orion's capability to fuse blockchain data with dark web intelligence in a single investigation. Key differentiator vs. Chainalysis (blockchain-only).

Investigation Case — Orion's case management layer. Investigators build cases, attach evidence, generate reports, and export SAR narratives from within Orion.

SAR Narrative — Suspicious Activity Report narrative. Orion auto-generates the written SAR narrative from investigation findings, reducing filing time from 4hrs to 20min.

Travel Rule Screening — VASP-to-VASP transfer compliance check. Orion screens counterparty wallets for sanctions and risk before funds move.

Threat Actor Attribution — Linking a crypto wallet or dark web activity to a known threat actor group (Lazarus, LockBit, ALPHV). Orion's AI does this automatically from patterns and intel fusion.

POV (Proof of Value) — TRM's preferred term for what others call a POC. 30-day structured evaluation on customer's own use cases. 92% POV-to-close rate.

TRM Entity Database — TRM's proprietary database of named entities (exchanges, mixers, darknets, sanctioned actors). Updated daily from LE intelligence and on-chain patterns.

Orion vs TRM Platform — TRM Platform = compliance screening (used by Chainalysis customers for comparison). Orion = AI investigation layer on top. A customer can use both — Orion is the investigation capability, TRM Platform is the compliance layer.

⛓️
Blockchain & Crypto (16)

Wallet Address — Public identifier for a crypto account. Like a bank account number but visible on the blockchain. Multiple addresses can belong to one entity.

UTXO (Unspent Transaction Output) — Bitcoin's accounting model. Each "coin" is an unspent output from a previous transaction. Tracing UTXOs = tracing bitcoin flows.

Gas Fee — Transaction fee on Ethereum-based chains. Paid in ETH. High gas = network congestion. Relevant for tracing: criminals pay fees that create on-chain fingerprints.

Smart Contract — Self-executing code on a blockchain (primarily Ethereum). DeFi protocols, NFTs, and token contracts are all smart contracts.

DeFi (Decentralised Finance) — Financial services on-chain with no intermediary. Uniswap (exchange), Aave (lending), Curve (stablecoin swaps). Used by criminals to swap tokens without KYC.

Mixer / Tumbler — Service that obfuscates transaction origin by pooling and redistributing funds. Tornado Cash (OFAC sanctioned), JoinMarket, Wasabi Wallet. Orion traces through mixers.

Bridge — Cross-chain transfer protocol. Criminals use bridges to move funds between chains (BTC → ETH → TRON) to break the tracing chain. Orion handles automated bridge tracing.

Exchange — Platform where crypto is bought/sold for fiat or other crypto. Centralised (Binance, Coinbase) or decentralised (Uniswap). CEXs have KYC; DEXs often don't.

VASP (Virtual Asset Service Provider) — Regulatory term for any crypto business: exchanges, wallets, custodians. Subject to FATF Travel Rule compliance obligations.

Stablecoin — Crypto pegged to fiat (USDT, USDC, DAI). USDT on Tron is the world's most used for illicit transactions due to low fees and speed.

Privacy Coin — Cryptocurrency with built-in anonymity (Monero/XMR, Zcash/ZEC). Harder to trace but not impossible. Orion has partial XMR tracing capability.

NFT (Non-Fungible Token) — Unique blockchain asset. Used in wash trading, money laundering, and sanctions evasion (sale of high-value NFTs between related wallets).

On-chain / Off-chain — On-chain = recorded on the blockchain (visible). Off-chain = not on the blockchain (P2P transfers, internal exchange ledgers). Orion works on on-chain data.

Mempool — Pool of unconfirmed transactions waiting to be added to the blockchain. Orion can monitor mempool for watched wallets — alerts before confirmation.

Cold Wallet — Crypto storage not connected to the internet (hardware wallet like Ledger). Harder to trace but funds must eventually move on-chain to be used.

Heuristic Clustering — Grouping multiple wallet addresses that likely belong to the same entity based on on-chain behaviour patterns. Core to how TRM builds entity graphs.

👤
Threat Actors (12)

Lazarus Group — North Korean state-sponsored hacking group (DPRK). Responsible for $3B+ in crypto theft since 2017. Primary target for US gov and LE Orion customers. Orion has dedicated Lazarus attribution models.

LockBit — Most prolific ransomware gang (RaaS model). Operated until Feb 2024 takedown, resurfaced as LockBit 3.0. Key use case for FBI, NCA, Europol Orion deployments.

ALPHV / BlackCat — Sophisticated RaaS group. Known for triple extortion (encrypt + leak + DDoS). FBI seized domains Dec 2023. Still active under variants.

REvil / Sodinokibi — Russian RaaS group behind Kaseya ($70M demand) and JBS attacks. Key example of Orion attribution capability for LE pitch.

APT (Advanced Persistent Threat) — State-sponsored or sophisticated long-term attacker. APT38 (DPRK financial crimes), APT29 (Cozy Bear, Russia), APT41 (China dual espionage/crime).

IAB (Initial Access Broker) — Criminal who sells network access to ransomware operators. Orion traces IAB dark web listings to real-world identity and infrastructure.

Ransomware-as-a-Service (RaaS) — Ransomware distributed as a franchise. Core developer + affiliates run attacks and split ransom. LockBit, ALPHV, BlackBasta all operate this way.

Pig Butchering (Sha Zhu Pan) — Long-term crypto romance scam. Victim cultivated over weeks/months before being defrauded. $75B+ industry. Major use case for Binance, Coinbase Orion deployments.

Money Mule — Person who transfers illegally obtained funds on behalf of a criminal. Orion maps mule networks from transaction patterns and dark web recruitment ads.

Bulletproof Hosting — Web hosting provider that ignores takedown requests. Criminal infrastructure is hosted here. Identified in Orion via IP/ASN patterns (Frantech, M247).

Sanctioned Entity — Individual, organisation, or wallet designated by OFAC, EU, UN, or national authorities. Transactions with sanctioned entities can trigger criminal liability.

Insider Threat — Employee who facilitates or conducts fraud/theft. Relevant for financial institution Orion deployments — detect anomalous employee crypto transactions.

🌑
Dark Web & OSINT (12)

Dark Web — Portion of the internet not indexed by search engines, accessible via Tor. Hosts criminal marketplaces, forums, ransomware leak sites, and stolen data shops.

Tor (.onion) — Anonymity network used to access dark web. .onion addresses are dark web sites. Orion automatically handles Tor-based intelligence gathering.

Dark Web Marketplace — Criminal e-commerce site selling drugs, stolen credentials, hacking tools, CSAM. Hydra (seized 2022), AlphaBay, Bohemia. Orion indexes marketplace activity.

Ransomware Leak Site — Dark web site where ransomware gangs publish stolen data from non-paying victims. LockBit, ALPHV, Cl0p all operate leak sites. Orion monitors these in real time.

OSINT (Open Source Intelligence) — Intelligence gathered from publicly available sources: social media, forums, registries, news, dark web. Orion fuses OSINT with blockchain and dark web data.

PGP Key — Encryption key used by dark web actors for secure communication. PGP keys are fingerprints — the same key appearing on different dark web forums links personas to the same actor.

Handle / Alias — Dark web forum username. Orion links handles across platforms and to real-world identities and wallet addresses.

Stealer Log — Dataset from infostealer malware containing victim credentials, cookies, and financial data. Sold on dark web markets. Flashpoint and SpyCloud index these.

Carding Forum — Dark web forum for buying/selling stolen payment card data. Often crypto-denominated transactions. Orion maps carding operation financial flows.

Darknet Intelligence (DNI) — Intelligence derived specifically from dark web sources. Orion provides DNI integrated into investigation workflows — no separate dark web tool needed.

HUMINT — Human intelligence. Law enforcement source reporting. IntSights/Flashpoint supplement automated dark web monitoring with HUMINT for financial crime specifically.

Attribution — The process of identifying the real-world individual or group behind a crypto wallet, dark web persona, or cyber attack. Orion's primary value: automated attribution at scale.

🏦
Financial Crime & AML (14)

AML (Anti-Money Laundering) — Regulatory framework requiring financial institutions to detect and report suspicious money flows. Primary driver for bank and exchange Orion purchases.

KYC (Know Your Customer) — Identity verification required at onboarding. Exchanges must KYC customers before allowing trading. Orion enhances KYC with wallet risk screening.

SAR (Suspicious Activity Report) — Report filed with FinCEN (US), NCA (UK), or equivalent when suspicious financial activity is detected. Orion auto-generates SAR narratives.

STR (Suspicious Transaction Report) — International equivalent of SAR. Filed with AUSTRAC (AU), FCA (UK), MAS (SG). Same Orion use case — auto-generation and investigation support.

PEP (Politically Exposed Person) — Government official or close associate. Subject to enhanced due diligence under AML regulations. Orion screens for PEP exposure via Dow Jones/World-Check.

EDD (Enhanced Due Diligence) — Deeper investigation required for high-risk customers (PEPs, high-value transactions, crypto businesses). Orion provides the investigation capability for EDD.

Travel Rule — FATF requirement that VASPs share originator/beneficiary information for transfers above threshold ($1000 US, €1000 EU). Orion screens counterparty wallets for Travel Rule compliance.

Layering — Second stage of money laundering. Moving funds through multiple accounts/transactions to obscure origin. In crypto: chain hops, DEX swaps, mixer usage. Orion traces layering automatically.

TBML (Trade-Based Money Laundering) — Using international trade transactions to move criminal funds. Involves over/under invoicing, ghost shipments. Orion cross-references trade data with blockchain flows.

Hawala — Informal money transfer network common in Middle East and South Asia. No physical money moves — based on trust and IOUs. Used alongside crypto for criminal fund transfers.

Sanctions Evasion — Using crypto to bypass OFAC/EU/UN sanctions. DPRK, Russia, Iran key use cases. VASPs can face $1B+ penalties for sanctions violations. Orion screens in real time.

FinCEN — US Financial Crimes Enforcement Network. Receives SARs from US banks and exchanges. Sets BSA (Bank Secrecy Act) AML requirements. Uses TRM data for enforcement.

FATF (Financial Action Task Force) — Intergovernmental body setting global AML standards. FATF grey/black list designation = regulatory crisis for the country. FATF Travel Rule = key VASP compliance driver.

Beneficial Owner — Real individual who ultimately controls or benefits from a company/account, even if another entity is the nominal owner. AML requires identification of beneficial owners. Orion links crypto to beneficial owners via OSINT.

⚖️
Regulatory & Compliance (12)

CIRCIA — Cyber Incident Reporting for Critical Infrastructure Act (US). Requires critical infrastructure operators to report cyber incidents to CISA within 72hrs. Creates urgent Orion demand from CISA, DHS, and CI sectors.

MiCA (Markets in Crypto Assets) — EU regulation for crypto asset service providers. In force 2024. Requires VASPs to comply with AML/Travel Rule. Major driver for European exchange Orion deals.

DORA (Digital Operational Resilience Act) — EU regulation requiring financial institutions to demonstrate cyber investigation capability. Effective Jan 2025. Creates Orion budget line for EU banks.

FedRAMP — US Federal Risk and Authorization Management Program. Cloud security certification required to sell software to US federal agencies. TRM holds FedRAMP Moderate — key differentiator for US gov deals.

OFAC (Office of Foreign Assets Control) — US Treasury division administering economic sanctions. OFAC SDN List = individuals/entities prohibited from US transactions. Orion reflects SDN updates within 2hrs.

NIS2 — EU Network and Information Security Directive 2. Extends cyber incident reporting obligations to more sectors than NIS1. Creates Orion demand from EU critical infrastructure.

SEC Cyber Disclosure Rules — SEC requires public companies to disclose material cyber incidents within 4 business days (Form 8-K). Forces companies to have investigation capability. Orion provides forensic evidence for disclosures.

AUSTRAC — Australian Transaction Reports and Analysis Centre. Australia's AML/CTF regulator. Fines: up to AUD $222M per contravention. Key driver for Australian bank and exchange deals.

BSA (Bank Secrecy Act) — US primary AML law. Requires banks and exchanges to file SARs, maintain KYC records, implement AML programmes. FinCEN enforces BSA compliance.

GDPR / Data Residency — EU privacy regulation. Affects where Orion can store investigation data for EU customers. TRM offers EU data residency configurations. Key blocker in EU deals — have the answer ready.

IL2 / IL4 / IL5 — US government data classification levels. IL2 = CUI, IL4 = CUI + DoD sensitive, IL5 = classified. Orion operates on unclassified data. Know these for US NatSec conversations.

G-Cloud / SEWP / CIO-SP3 — Procurement vehicles used by UK (G-Cloud) and US (SEWP, CIO-SP3) governments. TRM is on all three. "What contract vehicle would you use?" is always your first procurement question.

🔍
Investigation & Intelligence (10)

IOC (Indicator of Compromise) — Evidence that a system has been breached: suspicious IP, domain, wallet address, hash. Orion enriches IOCs with blockchain and dark web context automatically.

TTPs (Tactics, Techniques, Procedures) — How an attacker operates. MITRE ATT&CK framework documents TTPs. LockBit's TTPs include specific ransom wallet patterns Orion recognises.

MITRE ATT&CK — Framework cataloguing attacker TTPs. Used by SOC teams and LE. Orion maps investigation findings to MITRE ATT&CK automatically for reporting.

Chain of Custody — Legal documentation showing evidence was collected, preserved, and handled properly. Orion generates chain-of-custody reports for court-admissible evidence. Critical for LE deals.

Threat Intelligence Feed — Automated stream of IOCs and threat actor data from providers (Recorded Future, Flashpoint, Mandiant). Orion ingests multiple feeds and fuses them with on-chain data.

SIGINT / HUMINT / OSINT — Signals intelligence / Human intelligence / Open source intelligence. Orion operates in the OSINT layer (unclassified) and fuses with classified sources via API for IC customers.

Five Eyes — Intelligence alliance: US, UK, Canada, Australia, New Zealand. All Five Eyes agencies use TRM. Peer agency endorsement is the fastest door-opener for new Five Eyes LE deals.

Forensics — Digital investigation for legal proceedings. Disk forensics (Magnet, Cellebrite) ≠ Orion. Orion is on-chain forensics + dark web intelligence. Do NOT position Co-Case in forensics GTM (per March 25 GTM bulletin).

TTP Fingerprint — Unique combination of techniques that identifies a specific threat actor. E.g. DPRK Lazarus always uses specific mixer sequences. Orion has pre-built fingerprints for 200+ named groups.

Dwell Time — Time between initial compromise and detection. Average: 21 days for ransomware. Orion reduces investigation time, which reduces effective dwell time. Key ROI metric for LE and IR buyers.

⚔️
Competitive Landscape (8)

Chainalysis — Largest blockchain analytics company. Blockchain-only, no dark web, no AI investigation, no NLQ. Strong compliance brand. Orion beats on investigation depth + dark web fusion + AI speed. Often the incumbent.

Elliptic — Compliance-focused blockchain analytics. Weaker investigation capability than Chainalysis. Strong in European banking. Orion beats on everything beyond compliance screening. Rarely a direct competitor — more often a step-up from.

Palantir — Data analytics platform used by some LE agencies. General purpose, not crypto-native. Expensive, slow deployment, requires months of professional services. Orion beats on speed, crypto-native depth, and cost.

i2 Analyst Notebook (IBM) — Legacy 1990s link analysis visualisation tool. Manual data entry. Zero automation. No blockchain or dark web. Orion uses AI to generate what i2 analysts build manually. Often described as "i2's AI brain."

Maltego — OSINT graph tool. Manual — analysts build entity graphs by hand. No blockchain, no AI. Orion automates everything Maltego does manually in seconds. Common in LE and threat intel teams.

Recorded Future — Threat intelligence platform. Strong on traditional threat intel feeds (APT, CVE, geopolitical). Limited crypto intelligence. Orion beats on investigation capability and blockchain + dark web fusion. Often complementary.

Chainalysis Reactor — Chainalysis's investigation UI (vs KYT their compliance product). Manual blockchain tracing. No dark web. No AI. Reactor vs Orion is your most common demo-stage competitive situation.

Nansen / Glassnode — Blockchain analytics for crypto traders and investors. NOT competitive with Orion — they serve a completely different use case (market data, not crime investigation). If a prospect mentions these, they've confused the category.

🤝Partner Enablement CHANNEL PARTNERS · SEGMENT TALK TRACKS · OBJECTION HANDLING
From Patrick South / John Cann thread: partners lose confidence after 2 minutes. These segment-specific talk tracks let resellers (DataExpert, ForensicWorx, Moonsoft, KPMG, Deloitte, EY) pitch Orion without TRM on the call.

AI Morning Briefing
April 15, 2026

Market: EMEA financial services budgets accelerating. 4 new RFP signals detected across UK, Spain, Germany. DORA deadline creating urgency — your Q2 pipeline should focus here.

Threats: Ransomware targeting financial services up 23% this quarter. LockBit 4.0 variant emerging. Critical timing for compliance-driven pitches — use the "regulatory pressure" angle.

Pipeline: Deutsche Bank showing engagement signals (opened proposal 3x). NCA UK silent for 14 days — escalation recommended. JP Morgan POC results due Friday.

Competitive: Recorded Future launched new dark web module yesterday. Differentiate on investigation depth vs. their passive feeds. Update your demo to show the entity graph advantage.

This briefing updates daily based on your pipeline, market activity, and threat landscape.

🕸️Multi-Threading Scorecard DEAL RISK · COVERAGE MAP · ORION-SPECIFIC PLAYS
Single-threaded deals die at procurement. The person who loved your demo often cannot sign. Map your full coverage before you need it.
Orion Multi-Threading Playbook — By Vertical
🏛️ Law Enforcement
Your champion: Unit Commander / Lead Investigator
Thread 2 — Budget: CIO / IT Director / Contracting Officer
Thread 3 — Exec: ASAC / Deputy Director / SAC
Thread 4 — Technical: Digital Forensics Lead / IT Security

Orion-specific play: Invite the IT Director to a separate technical briefing on FedRAMP compliance and air-gap deployment — this separates Orion from "another analytics tool" in budget conversations. The ASAC layer typically moves budget; brief them on ROI (investigation hours saved × analyst cost).

Signal to multi-thread: Champion says "I love it but I need to run it by..." — get the name immediately.
🏦 Financial Services
Your champion: Head of Financial Intelligence / AML Investigations Lead
Thread 2 — Compliance: CMLRO / CCO / Chief Compliance Officer
Thread 3 — Tech: CISO / Head of RegTech / IT Security
Thread 4 — Legal: GC / DPO (for GDPR/data residency sign-off)

Orion-specific play: The CMLRO cares about examiner relationships and MiCA/DORA deadlines — brief them separately on regulatory compliance positioning. DPO must be engaged early if data residency is in scope (EU customers especially). GC needed for MSA redline — engage them in week 2 of POC, not week 4.

Red flag: Only talking to the AML analyst — they love the tool but cannot sign. You need the CCO for $500K+ decisions.
🔄 Crypto Exchange
Your champion: Head of Compliance / Compliance Lead
Thread 2 — Legal/Reg: CCO / General Counsel / Regulatory Affairs
Thread 3 — Tech: CTO / Head of Engineering (API integration)
Thread 4 — Exec: CEO / COO (for strategic + budget decisions)

Orion-specific play: Exchanges have a VASP licensing / regulatory layer above the compliance function — brief the CCO separately on how Orion demonstrates proactive investigation capability to regulators (MAS, FCA, SEC). CTO engagement is critical for API integration timelines — get them in the POC kickoff, not after agreement.

Common single-thread trap: Head of Compliance loves Orion; CEO/COO is focused on TRM Screening costs. They are different budget conversations. Run them in parallel.
🏛️ Regulator / NatSec
Your champion: Financial Intelligence Director / Lead Analyst
Thread 2 — Budget: Deputy Director / Division Chief
Thread 3 — Contracts: Procurement Officer / Contracting
Thread 4 — Legal: GC / IT Security / CISO

Orion-specific play: Regulators require sole-source justification for non-competed contracts — brief the Contracting Officer early and get TRM registered as approved vendor. The Deputy Director / Division Chief needs ROI framing: how many more investigations closed, how much analyst time saved. Security review by CISO is standard; have our FedRAMP documentation ready at week 1.

Procurement trap: Budget cycles are annual. If you miss the submission window, you wait 12 months. Identify the budget cycle deadline in discovery call 1.
Coverage Health Check
🔴 Single Thread
1 contact · 1 level
Deal at high risk
Champion can block you
🟡 Partial Coverage
2–3 contacts · 2 levels
Risk remains
Economic buyer unknown
🟢 Well Covered
4+ contacts · 3+ levels
Economic buyer confirmed
Procurement pre-engaged
🔍Competitor Movement Tracker LIVE INTEL · CHAINALYSIS · ELLIPTIC · PALANTIR
Track competitor news, product moves, and pricing. Know what Chainalysis is pitching before your customer does.
Chainalysis Weaknesses
• Blockchain-only — no dark web OSINT
• No AI investigation reasoning layer
• Manual analyst workflow — 40-200h per case
• No NLQ — requires trained specialists
• Crypto-only (no fiat, no OSINT fusion)
• No cross-case entity linking at scale
• Reactor UI described as "complex" by users
Elliptic Weaknesses
• Compliance-only (screening, not investigation)
• No investigation workflow or report generation
• No dark web intelligence
• Strong in transaction monitoring — weak in attribution
• No AI-native investigation
• Limited LE customer base
• Smaller threat actor coverage vs TRM
Palantir / i2 Weaknesses
• No crypto-native intelligence
• Requires data science team to configure
• 12-18 month implementation cycle
• Expensive: $5-30M enterprise contracts
• No blockchain tracing capability
• Orion pitch: "Palantir+Chainalysis costs 10x Orion alone"
Orion Differentiators
• AI-native: Co-Case Agent reasons across sources
• NLQ: analysts query in plain English
• Dark web + blockchain + OSINT fusion
• Automated investigation reports (SAR-ready)
• TRM data advantage: 1T+ transactions, 1000+ threat actor profiles
• Fastest time-to-value: meaningful output day one

📌 SAVED OUTPUTS
⚡ WELCOME TO APERTURE
Orion AI Sales Intelligence · Select your profile to personalise everything
1. Select Your Profile
Click 👤 Select Profile in the top nav. This personalises AI responses, auto-fills your accounts, and loads your pipeline context into every feature.
2. Start with Command Center
Your home screen. Auto-loads deal health, account news, and your activity. Use it to start every day and see what needs attention.
3. Ask the Chat Anything
The AI sidebar answers any Orion sales question. Try: "Pitch strategy for FBI" or "How do I handle the Chainalysis objection?". Use Pitch / Advisor / Research / Investigate modes.
4. Open a War Room
Before any customer call, type the account name in ⚔️ War Room. Gets you meeting brief, Gong intel, DealBot summary, competitive traps — all in 90 seconds.
Built for TRM Labs Orion sales team · 78 active deals · 78 AI features · 53 tabs
